its own logic, worse prospects. The \eur{\nbElevenBValuePerVisit} visit value and the
\eur{\nbElevenBConvMargin} margin per conversion are finance inputs, swept but assumed: the
secondary conversion rung puts an exposure at \eur{\nbElevenBConvValue} on the full-file anchor's
\nbElevenBAnchorConvWald{} pp, and at a \pc{\nbElevenBBaseConv} base rate it is honestly noisy.
And the estimate is a blend across several incrementality tests: fine for a benchmark cap, to be
blurred into one campaign's plan with care.

\begin{remark}[What the experiment bought]
Notice what the randomized lockout bought. It made \cref{as:iv:exog} true by construction,
\cref{as:iv:mono} true by construction, and \cref{as:iv:excl} defensible in a sentence: a silent
lockout does nothing to a user who never learns of it. Every hard problem in this chapter was solved
\emph{in the design}, before any data was analysed. That is the deeper reason to run incrementality
tests at all: not to produce a number, but to make the number identifiable \citep{lewis2015,
gordon2019}.
\end{remark}

% =====================================================================================
\section{Takeaways}
\label{sec:iv:take}

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*, itemsep=.45em]
  \item \textbf{Endogeneity is a correlation with the \emph{full} error, and adjustment cannot
        touch it.} \Cref{eq:iv:endog} is stated against $\kappa U + \varepsilon$, not against
        $\varepsilon$: the leak is the unobserved intent, and controlling for what was measured is
        powerless against a confounder that was not. An instrument does not adjust for $U$. It finds
        a slice of exposure that $U$ could not have caused.

  \item \textbf{IV identifies a LATE, and the instrument defines the population.} \Cref{eq:iv:late}
        is the effect on \emph{compliers}: \nbElevenFsShift{} points of these customers, the ones
        the encouragement moves. Always-takers and never-takers are absent from the estimate, not
        merely down-weighted. A different instrument therefore estimates a different quantity, and
        two valid instruments disagreeing is not a contradiction. Report the scope.

  \item \textbf{The first-stage F is a precondition, and \cref{eq:iv:oneoverF} tells you what it
        buys.} At $F = \nbElevenFStat$ the residual OLS-bias leakage is \eur{\nbElevenFLeak} per
        exposure; on Criteo, at $F = \nbElevenBFStat$ (\nbElevenBFOverBar$\times$ the bar) it
        is \nbElevenBFLeak{} pp. In both cases the diagnostic \emph{certifies}. Below 10 it does the
        opposite: \cref{tab:iv:weak} shows a single draw's 90\,\% range spanning \eur{\nbElevenWeakLo}
        to \eur{\nbElevenWeakHi} at $F \approx \nbElevenWeakFWorst$, with the median drifting back
        toward OLS. A weak instrument is worse than none.

  \item \textbf{The hand-rolled 2SLS standard error is too WIDE, not too narrow.} \Cref{eq:iv:ssr}
        is the proof and \cref{tab:iv:se} the measurement: \nbElevenSeHandRatio$\times$ the correct
        one. Regressing $Y$ on $\hat X$ charges the estimate for first-stage prediction error that is
        not sampling error at all. The folklore says the opposite; the folklore is wrong.

  \item \textbf{Precision is not accuracy.} The tightest interval in this chapter belongs, in both
        the simulation and the real data, to the biased estimator: a standard error of
        \eur{\nbElevenNaiveSe} on a number \eur{\nbElevenNaiveErr} wrong, and \nbElevenBNaiveSe{} pp
        on one \nbElevenBNaiveErr{} pp wrong, around an anchor its own interval excludes. More
        data shrinks that interval around the same wrong number, forever.

  \item \textbf{Classical and Bayesian agree, and on Criteo they \emph{coincide}.} On
        \nbElevenBNFit{} rows the posterior and 2SLS land \nbElevenBAgreePp{} pp apart
        (\nbElevenBAgreeSes{} standard errors), with interval widths in a ratio of
        \nbElevenBWidthRatio$\times$. That is Bernstein--von Mises in a print-out: the posterior
        \emph{is} the sampling distribution the delta method wrote in closed form. Which of the two
        sits nearer the anchor differs by \nbElevenBErrGapSes{} standard errors, and this chapter
        refuses to call a winner on that.

  \item \textbf{Where Bayes lost, the culprit was a default \emph{specification}, not the paradigm,
        and it was a \emph{prior}, not the likelihood.} As CausalPy ships it, the Bayesian arm carries
        \eur{\nbElevenGaussSeedBias} of bias and \pc{\nbElevenGaussSeedCovPct} coverage of a
        nominal-90\,\% interval (\cref{tab:iv:likelihoods}). The obvious suspect is the
        linear-probability likelihood, and it is innocent: that likelihood's own maximiser is 2SLS,
        exactly, so it contributes no bias to $\beta$. The mild prior on the endogeneity correlation
        is innocent too (flatten it and the estimate does not move, \eur{\nbElevenGaussFlatMean} against
        \eur{\nbElevenGaussMean}). The guilty component is the default prior on the error
        \emph{scales}, calibrated for a unit-scale variable and left to meet sales in the tens; diffuse
        it and the posterior mean collapses onto 2SLS (\cref{app:iv:forensic}). Respecifying the model
        (the probit repair of \cref{eq:iv:probit}) brings the bias to \eur{\nbElevenSeedBias} and
        coverage to \pc{\nbElevenSeedCovPct}. A mis-scaled default prior is a specification error in
        exactly the sense a misspecified likelihood is: the analyst's mistake, not Bayes's, and the
        Bayesian arm is also what caught and priced it.

  \item \textbf{What the posterior adds here is the decision, and only the decision.} Corrected,
        it bought no accuracy (a tie inside \eur{\nbElevenTieTol}), no precision, no diagnostic and
        no robustness. What it bought is
        $\Prob(\beta > c)$, which the classical column prints as \emph{not defined}, and
        bootstrapping the Wald would not fix that, because a frequency over datasets is not a belief
        about $\beta$. The euro cap of \cref{eq:iv:rule} is a \textbf{posterior quantile}, a 90\,\%
        \emph{probability} bar and never a confidence bar. \textbf{At Criteo scale the Bayesian layer
        is a convenience, not a rescue: absent the need for $\Prob(\text{effect} > \text{cost})$, the
        classical baseline alone would have been a complete and defensible analysis.}

  \item \textbf{The exclusion restriction is untestable, and that is why you price it.}
        \Cref{eq:iv:excl_stress} bounds the leak that would flip the call: \eur{\nbElevenBreakDelta}
        of a \eur{\nbElevenReduced} reduced form in the simulation, \nbElevenBDeltaStar{} pp of a
        \nbElevenBReduced{} pp one on Criteo. Defend it on design, not on data: a content-free
        serving lottery is an instrument, and an encouragement email that shows the product is not.
\end{enumerate}

\notebooknote{%
  \Crefrange{sec:iv:dgm}{sec:iv:wrong} are \code{notebooks/11\_endogenous\_exposure\_iv.ipynb}
  (simulator \code{cmp.dgp.iv\_ad\_exposure}, seed \nbElevenSeed, $n = \nbElevenN$);
  \cref{sec:iv:real} is \code{notebooks/11b\_endogenous\_exposure\_real\_data.ipynb}, which loads
  Criteo's published incrementality panel \citep{diemert2018} directly and computes its
  \nbElevenBNFull-row anchor with \code{cmp.data.criteo\_full\_anchor}. Both run in a fast teaching
  mode (\code{CMP\_FAST=1}, short chains) and a full mode (\code{CMP\_FAST=0}); every number in this
  chapter comes from the full run, emitted by \code{cmp.report} and injected here as a macro. The
  Bayesian arm of \crefrange{sec:iv:bayes}{sec:iv:euro} is fitted twice: first as CausalPy's
  joint-Gaussian instrumental-variable model (\cref{eq:iv:joint}), then as the binary-treatment
  probit of \cref{eq:iv:probit} (\code{cmp.estimators.iv\_binary\_treatment}), which is the posterior
  the chapter reports and spends. \Cref{sec:iv:real}'s posterior is the joint Gaussian, where at
  Criteo's $n$ the difference is immaterial. The classical arm is \code{cmp.classical.iv\_2sls}.
  \citet{angrist2009} remains the standard applied reference for everything in this chapter, and
  \citet{imbens2014} the standard survey.}

% =====================================================================================
\clearpage
\section*{Appendix 13.A\quad Formal machinery}
\label{app:iv:forensic}
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Appendix 13.A\quad Formal machinery}

The body states each idea in plain language, gives the euro result, and points here. The first seven
subsections are the formal reference (the estimand, the two estimators, the weak-instrument
diagnostic, the LATE theorem, the two likelihoods, and the large-sample theorem that ties the
posterior to the classical answer), each with the displayed formula the body omits and a worked
example where one is natural. The next group (13.A.8 to 13.A.12) is the forensic promised in
\cref{sec:iv:valid}: \emph{which} component of the default Bayesian model biased it, established by
refitting each alternative in turn. The last four (13.A.13 to 13.A.16) are worked examples of the
Session-5 ideas the body names but does not need: weak-instrument-robust inference (Anderson--Rubin),
profiling the compliers (Abadie's $\kappa$), attrition (Lee bounds), and the four faces of
endogeneity.

\subsection*{13.A.1\quad The just-identified estimand and the Wald estimator}

With a single binary instrument $Z$, a single binary endogenous treatment $X$ and no controls, the
model is \emph{just-identified}: one instrument for one endogenous regressor. The estimand is the
complier LATE of \cref{eq:iv:late}, and the estimator is the Wald ratio of \cref{eq:iv:wald}: the
reduced form divided by the first stage. Under \crefrange{as:iv:rel}{as:iv:mono} that ratio equals
the average effect among compliers: the reduced form is $\Prob(\text{complier})\cdot\beta_{\text{LATE}}$
and the first stage is $\Prob(\text{complier})$, so their quotient is $\beta_{\text{LATE}}$.

Because the estimator is a ratio of two noisy quantities, its variance is the delta method:
%
\begin{equation}
  \Var\big(\hat\beta\big) \;\approx\;
    \frac{1}{\hat\pi^{2}}\Var\big(\hat\tau\big)
  \;+\; \frac{\hat\beta^{2}}{\hat\pi^{2}}\Var\big(\hat\pi\big)
  \;-\; \frac{2\hat\beta}{\hat\pi^{2}}\operatorname{Cov}\big(\hat\tau,\ \hat\pi\big),
  \label{eq:iv:delta}
\end{equation}
%
the three terms being the numerator's noise, the denominator's noise, and their correlation. When
the first stage is strong the denominator's relative noise is $1/\sqrt F$, the last two terms nearly
vanish, and the one-term shortcut $\text{SE} \approx \text{SE}(\hat\tau)/\hat\pi$ is exact enough to
use, as \cref{sec:iv:real} audits on the Criteo folds, where it agrees with the full expression to
\pc{\nbElevenBSeShortGap}.

\subsection*{13.A.2\quad Two-stage least squares and the control-function form}

Two-stage least squares fits the first stage $X = b_0 + \pi Z$, keeps the fitted $\hat X$, and
regresses $Y$ on $\hat X$. An algebraically identical route is the \textbf{control function}: fit the
same first stage, keep its residual $\hat v = X - \hat X$, and regress the outcome on the
\emph{actual} treatment with that residual added as a control,
%
\begin{equation}
  Y \;=\; \beta_0 \;+\; \beta X \;+\; \phi\,\hat v \;+\; \text{noise}.
  \label{eq:iv:cf}
\end{equation}
%
The residual $\hat v$ carries the part of exposure that intent drove; conditioning on it purges the
endogeneity, and the coefficient $\beta$ in \cref{eq:iv:cf} equals the 2SLS coefficient exactly. With
one binary instrument and one binary treatment both also equal the Wald ratio (\cref{prop:iv:wald}).

The recipe has one trap, in the standard error rather than the coefficient. The correct residual is
formed against the real regressor, $e = y - Xb$; the hand-rolled second stage forms it against the
fitted one, and the two differ by the first-stage prediction error:
%
\begin{equation}
  \underbrace{y - \hat X b}_{\text{naive residual}}
  \;=\; \underbrace{(y - Xb)}_{e,\ \text{correct}}
      \;+\; \underbrace{(X - \hat X)\,b}_{\text{first-stage prediction error}} .
  \label{eq:iv:resid}
\end{equation}
%
The fitted regressor $\hat X$ is the projection of $X$ onto the instrument space, so the IV residual
$e$ is orthogonal to $\hat X$ by the normal equations but \emph{not} to $X$ itself. Squaring
\cref{eq:iv:resid} term by term therefore leaves \emph{three} pieces, not two, with a surviving cross
term:
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{SSR}_{\text{naive}} \;=\; \text{SSR}_{\text{correct}}
    \;+\; b^{2}\!\sum_i (X_i - \hat X_i)^{2}
    \;+\; 2\,b\!\sum_i e_i X_i .
  \label{eq:iv:ssr}
\end{equation}
%
The middle piece is a sum of squares and never negative. The last is the cross term, up to scale the
sample covariance between the IV residual and exposure. In this chapter's positively-confounded
market $\operatorname{Cov}(X, U) > 0$, so with $b > 0$ the cross term is positive too, both extra
pieces push the same way, and the hand-rolled interval comes out too \emph{wide}: the
\nbElevenSeHandRatio$\times$ inflation of \cref{sec:iv:classical}, \eur{\nbElevenSeHand} against the
correct \eur{\nbElevenSeCorrect}. The sign is not a law: once controls, several instruments or
negative endogeneity enter, the cross term can flip.

\subsection*{13.A.3\quad The first-stage F and the weak-instrument bound}

For a single instrument the first-stage F is the ordinary F-statistic of the first-stage regression,
\cref{eq:iv:F}, with $R^2$ the share of exposure variance the instrument explains. The $F > 10$
convention \citep{staiger1997, stock2005} is not superstition. A standard approximation prices the
residual 2SLS bias, relative to the OLS bias it was hired to remove, at $1/F$ (\cref{eq:iv:oneoverF}),
so $F = 10$ means at most a tenth of the OLS bias leaks back in. The bound is cashable in euros: here
OLS is off by \eur{\nbElevenNaiveErr}, and at $F = \nbElevenFStat$ the residual leakage is
\eur{\nbElevenFLeak} per exposure. That is negligible, which is why the small residual bias
\cref{sec:iv:valid} finds cannot be blamed on the instrument. Three refinements belong in a
practitioner's toolkit: the Montiel Olea--Pflueger effective F under heteroskedasticity
\citep{olea2013}, the \citet{kleibergen2006} rank test for several endogenous regressors, and the
reading of $F > 10$ as necessary, not sufficient \citep{andrews2019}.

\subsection*{13.A.4\quad The LATE theorem and monotonicity}

Decompose the reduced form over the four types. Randomization (\cref{as:iv:exog}) makes the
encouraged and un-encouraged groups comparable; exclusion (\cref{as:iv:excl}) silences always- and
never-takers, for whom $X(1) = X(0)$; monotonicity (\cref{as:iv:mono}) deletes the defier term. What
survives is \cref{eq:iv:itt}: the reduced form is $\Prob(\text{complier})\cdot\beta_{\text{LATE}}$,
and dividing by the first stage (itself the complier share) returns the LATE.

Monotonicity is the one assumption of the four with no observable footprint, so it is worth seeing
why the simulator satisfies it. Each customer draws \emph{one} latent willingness
$V_i \sim U(0,1)$ and takes the ad whenever her propensity clears it:
%
\begin{equation}
  X_i(z) \;=\; \mathbf 1\Big\{\, V_i \;<\; \sigma\big(\alpha_0 + \gamma z + \lambda U_i\big) \,\Big\}.
  \label{eq:iv:vocab_latent}
\end{equation}
%
The same $V_i$ is compared against \emph{both} propensities. A positive $\gamma$ can only raise the
propensity from $z=0$ to $z=1$, so a customer who took the ad unencouraged still takes it encouraged:
no defiers, by construction (\cref{fig:iv:monotone}). This is a statement about the \emph{joint} law
of $\big(X(0), X(1)\big)$, not either marginal alone. Draw the two potential treatments as
\emph{independent} coin flips at the same two marginal probabilities and defiers reappear in numbers,
with the first stage (a difference of marginals) entirely unchanged. The same observable data,
two different worlds; monotonicity is the choice between them, and no dataset can make it, because
each customer reveals only one of her two potential treatments.

\subsection*{13.A.5\quad The joint-Gaussian likelihood and its MLE}

The library model of \cref{eq:iv:joint} fits the first stage and the outcome equation jointly, with
correlated errors. It factors into a marginal model for $X$ and a control-function regression of $Y$
on $X$ and the first-stage residual: exactly \cref{eq:iv:cf}. For a just-identified linear IV the
maximiser of that likelihood \emph{is} two-stage least squares: the maximum-likelihood $\beta$ and
the 2SLS $\beta$ are the same number to machine precision, on binary $X$ and all. The notebook
asserts this rather than asking for faith. It forms the control-function estimate of
\cref{eq:iv:cf} and checks $|\hat\beta_{\text{CF}} - \hat\beta_{\text{2SLS}}| < 10^{-8}$ on the
chapter's data; across \nbElevenCfSeeds{} fresh panels the largest gap is \nbElevenCfMaxdiff.
Whatever the linear-probability row does to the \emph{support} of $X$, it contributes zero bias to
$\beta$. This is the fact the forensic subsections below turn on.

\subsection*{13.A.6\quad The probit first stage}

The binary-aware repair of \cref{eq:iv:probit,eq:iv:probit_out} replaces the Gaussian first-stage row
with a latent index crossing a threshold, keeping the outcome equation and letting the two errors
correlate. The likelihood factors honestly, a continuous outcome times a binary first stage:
%
\begin{equation}
  f(y, t) \;=\;
  \underbrace{\mathcal N\big(e;\ 0,\ \sigma^{2}\big)}_{\text{outcome equation}} \;\times\;
  \underbrace{\Prob(T = t \mid e)}_{\text{first stage, conditioned on } e},
  \qquad e = y - \beta_0 - \beta t,
  \label{eq:iv:probit_fact}
\end{equation}
%
and conditioning the index error on the outcome error carries the confounding through in one joint
model, with no plug-in residual:
%
\begin{equation}
  \Prob(T = 1 \mid e) \;=\;
  \Phi\!\left( \frac{\alpha_0 + \alpha_1 Z + \rho\,e/\sigma}{\sqrt{1 - \rho^{2}}} \right).
  \label{eq:iv:probit_phi}
\end{equation}
%
Set $\rho = 0$ and \cref{eq:iv:probit_phi} collapses to an ordinary probit first stage fitted in
isolation, the estimator that ignores the endogeneity; the $\rho\,e/\sigma$ term is the correction.
Unlike \cref{eq:iv:joint}, this likelihood cannot predict an exposure of $1.4$, because a Bernoulli
cannot.

\subsection*{13.A.7\quad Bernstein--von Mises}

With a well-specified likelihood and enough data, the posterior stops depending on the prior and
concentrates on the maximum-likelihood estimate, with the same sampling variance. Formally, the
posterior for $\beta$ is asymptotically normal around the MLE $\hat\beta$, with the inverse Fisher
information as variance:
%
\begin{equation}
  p(\beta \mid \text{data}) \;\xrightarrow{\;d\;}\;
  \mathcal N\!\big(\hat\beta,\ I(\beta)^{-1}/n\big),
  \label{eq:iv:bvm}
\end{equation}
%
so the posterior standard deviation and the classical standard error estimate the same quantity
\citep{vandervaart1998, gelman2013}. This is why, on \cref{sec:iv:real}'s hundred thousand Criteo
rows, the posterior and the delta method agree to \nbElevenBAgreePp{} pp with interval widths in a
ratio of \nbElevenBWidthRatio$\times$: the theorem showing up in a print-out.

\subsection*{13.A.8\quad The forensic: the identification is clean}

\input{build/floats/nb11_forensic_ablation}

The rest of this appendix establishes \emph{which} component of the default Bayesian model biased it,
by refitting each alternative in turn (\cref{fig:iv:ablation}). The order is a process of
elimination: first the identification, then the likelihood, then each prior. The Wald ratio \emph{is}
the IV identification with nothing else attached: two least-squares slopes and a division, no prior,
no likelihood, no sampler. Re-run it on fresh draws at growing $n$ and whatever bias it does
\emph{not} show cannot belong to the identification.

\input{build/tables/nb11_waldn}

It shows none (\cref{tab:iv:waldn}). The closed-form Wald stays pinned on the planted truth at
every sample size (a bias of \eur{\nbElevenWaldBiasBig} at $n = 10{,}000$) while its spread
shrinks like $1/\sqrt{n}$. \Cref{eq:iv:oneoverF} has already priced the pure IV leakage at
\eur{\nbElevenFLeak}, an order of magnitude smaller than anything in \cref{tab:iv:likelihoods}. The
identification is clean. So the Gaussian fit's bias belongs to the Bayesian apparatus, and the
apparatus has two parts, the \emph{likelihood} and the \emph{prior}. It is worth being careful
about which, because the obvious answer is wrong and the book's own thesis rides on getting it right.

\subsection*{13.A.9\quad Suspect one: the likelihood, and it is innocent}

\textbf{It is not the likelihood.} The joint-Gaussian model of \cref{eq:iv:joint} factors into a
model for $X$ and a control-function regression of $Y$ on $X$ and the first-stage residual. For
a just-identified linear IV, the maximiser of that likelihood \emph{is} two-stage least squares,
exactly. Not approximately: the maximum-likelihood $\beta$ and the 2SLS $\beta$ are the same number,
to machine precision, on binary $X$ and all. Whatever the linear-probability model does to the
\emph{support} of $X$ (and \cref{fig:iv:ppc} shows it does something real), it contributes
\emph{zero} bias to $\beta$. A likelihood whose own MLE is the unbiased classical estimator cannot be
the source of a bias in the posterior mean.

\subsection*{13.A.10\quad Suspect two: the prior on the endogeneity, and it is innocent}

\textbf{Nor is it the prior on the endogeneity.} The tempting second guess is the mild shrinkage the
covariance prior puts on the treatment--outcome correlation $\rho$. The notebook tests it directly,
by flattening that prior completely (a uniform law on $\rho$) and refitting everything else
unchanged. The posterior mean moves from \eur{\nbElevenGaussMean} to \eur{\nbElevenGaussFlatMean}.
That is to say, it does not move, and the coverage stays at \pc{\nbElevenGaussFlatCov}. At this
sample size the correlation prior is doing nothing, exactly as the spine of this book would predict.

\subsection*{13.A.11\quad Suspect three: the prior on the error scales, and it is guilty}

\textbf{What is left is the prior on the error \emph{scales}}: the one nuisance an analyst never
thinks about. The default prior on the two residual standard deviations is calibrated for a variable
on the unit scale, which is right for the 0/1 treatment and badly wrong for sales measured in the
tens; it pulls the outcome-error scale toward a value the data would never choose, and through the
joint model that mis-scaling tilts $\beta$ upward. Diffuse that one prior, holding everything else
fixed, and the posterior mean falls from \eur{\nbElevenGaussMean} to
\eur{\nbElevenGaussScalediffuseMean}, collapsing onto the 2SLS answer of \eur{\nbElevenIvEst}
(\cref{fig:iv:ablation}). That is the whole of it: not the likelihood, not the correlation prior, but
a default scale prior meeting data it was never calibrated for.

\begin{intuition}{This is the spine, not a counterexample to it}
The book's claim is that in these problems the prior does almost nothing and the likelihood is the
whole ballgame, and here is a chapter where the prior visibly bit. The two are reconciled by being
precise about what ``the prior mattering'' means. This is not a prior encoding a defensible belief and
that belief moving the answer; it is a \emph{default} prior, left unexamined, that is simply
\emph{mis-scaled} for the data it met. That is a specification error, and it sits in exactly the same
category as a misspecified likelihood: the analyst's mistake, not the paradigm's. The lesson of the
chapter is unchanged and, if anything, sharpened: \textbf{a Bayesian model is a likelihood
\emph{and} a set of priors, and either one, left on its defaults, can quietly bias a decision.} The
posterior predictive check of \cref{fig:iv:ppc} remains a genuine diagnostic of the support violation;
it simply is not the diagnosis of \emph{this} bias. Convicting the wrong component (blaming the
vivid likelihood violation for a bias that the quiet, mis-scaled error prior actually caused) is
the exact error the whole book warns against.
\end{intuition}

\subsection*{13.A.12\quad What survives the repair: finite-sample, or the wrong link?}

A small upward bias does survive, and it is reported rather than laundered. It has two possible
homes. If it is \emph{finite-sample}, it must shrink as $n$ grows. If it is the price of
approximating the simulator's \emph{logistic} assignment (\cref{eq:iv:first}) with a probit link, it
is structural and would not shrink at all. The test is obvious, and the notebook runs it: refit
\cref{eq:iv:probit} on \nbElevenProbitBignSeeds{} fresh panels at $n = \nbElevenProbitBignN$. The
bias falls from \eur{\nbElevenSeedBias} to \eur{\nbElevenProbitBiasBign}, with a standard error on
that mean of \eur{\nbElevenProbitBiasBignSe}. It shrinks, though not sharply, so the leftover is
mostly finite-sample, and a small residual link-approximation component cannot be excluded on that
many panels. At $n = \nbElevenN$, a little finite-sample bias is the price of admission; the decision
in \cref{sec:iv:euro} never turns on the last euro, which is exactly why it survives it.

\subsection*{13.A.13\quad Weak-instrument-robust inference: the Anderson--Rubin set}

The Wald interval divides by the first stage, so it inherits that denominator's noise and stops
being trustworthy just when the instrument is weak. The Anderson--Rubin (AR) confidence set avoids
the division \citep{anderson1949}. Fix a candidate effect $b_0$, form the adjusted outcome
$Y - b_0 X$, and test whether the instrument still predicts it:
%
\begin{equation}
  \text{AR}(b_0): \quad \text{regress } (Y - b_0 X) \text{ on } Z, \text{ and test } H_0:\ \pi_Z = 0 .
  \label{eq:iv:ar}
\end{equation}
%
At the true $b_0$ the adjusted outcome is $\beta_0 + \kappa U + \varepsilon$, which contains no $Z$
because the instrument is exogenous, so the coefficient is zero in expectation. The AR set is every
$b_0$ the test does not reject. \Cref{fig:iv:ar} plots the statistic across $b_0$ and shades the
90\,\% set, $[\eur{\nbElevenArLo}, \eur{\nbElevenArHi}]$. Here it nearly matches the Wald interval
$[\eur{\nbElevenIvLo}, \eur{\nbElevenIvHi}]$, because the first stage is strong. The point is the
regime where they differ: when the first stage is weak the Wald interval is unreliable and the AR
set is still exact, because it never divides by a near-zero first stage.

\input{build/floats/nb11_ar}

\subsection*{13.A.14\quad Abadie's $\kappa$: profiling the invisible compliers}

The LATE speaks for compliers, and \cref{sec:iv:vocab} showed that no row of data is labelled a
complier. Abadie's $\kappa$ recovers complier averages all the same \citep{abadie2003kappa}. With a
randomized instrument and $\Prob(Z{=}1) = p_z$,
%
\begin{equation}
  \kappa_i \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{X_i(1 - Z_i)}{1 - p_z} \;-\; \frac{(1 - X_i)\,Z_i}{p_z},
  \qquad
  \E[\,g \mid \text{complier}\,] \;=\; \frac{\E[\kappa\, g]}{\E[\kappa]},
  \label{eq:iv:kappa}
\end{equation}
%
for any characteristic $g$. The weights average to the complier share, $\E[\kappa] =
\pc{\nbElevenAbadieShare}$, which is the first stage again. \Cref{fig:iv:abadie} profiles the
compliers by their latent intent $U$. The $\kappa$-weighted mean, \nbElevenAbUKappa, lands on the
answer-key complier mean, \nbElevenAbUTrue, while the raw sample average, \nbElevenAbUAll, hides it.
The simulator lets us check $\kappa$ against the truth. On real data $\kappa$ is the only way to
describe who the estimate speaks for, because the compliers can never be listed.

\input{build/floats/nb11_abadie}

\subsection*{13.A.15\quad Attrition, and a nod to Lee bounds}

Every estimate above assumed the outcome is observed for everyone. Attrition breaks that. If who
reports depends on the treatment, the observed sample is selected, and a randomized instrument does
not repair it by itself. Lee bounds put an assumption-light bracket around the effect
\citep{lee2009}. Suppose the encouragement raises the reporting rate, so selection is monotone in
$Z$. Trim the over-retained arm by the excess-reporting fraction $q = (s_1 - s_0)/s_1$, from the top
for one bound and from the bottom for the other. What remains brackets the effect among the
always-reporters, the users who report whatever the encouragement. \Cref{fig:iv:lee} runs this on an
attrition overlay, not the main model. The encouraged report at \pc{\nbElevenLeeSone} against
\pc{\nbElevenLeeSzero}, and reporting also rises with sales, so the observed sample is selected on
the outcome. Trimming \pc{\nbElevenLeeTrim} brackets the effect between \eur{\nbElevenLeeLo} and
\eur{\nbElevenLeeHi} on randomization and monotone selection alone. The naive difference on
reporters, \eur{\nbElevenLeeNaive}, is biased by the selection, against the \eur{\nbElevenLeeItt}
full-sample intent-to-treat effect. The bounds are wide, and they are honest.

\input{build/floats/nb11_lee}

\subsection*{13.A.16\quad The four faces of endogeneity}

Endogeneity is any correlation between the treatment and the error. It arrives in four ways, and
this chapter treats one of them. \Cref{fig:iv:faces} shows all four, each a small world where a
plain OLS slope misses the truth:
%
\begin{description}[leftmargin=*, style=nextline]
  \item[Omitted variable, or selection.] This chapter's case: a hidden $U$ drives both treatment and
    outcome, so OLS credits the ad with the buyer's own intent. It reads \eur{\nbElevenFaceOvb}
    against a true 15.
  \item[Simultaneity, or reverse causation.] Price and quantity clear together, so each moves the
    other. OLS reads \nbElevenFaceSim{} against a true demand slope of $-1.5$. This is
    \cref{ch:pe}'s running example, and its cure is the same instrument.
  \item[Measurement error.] A noisily measured regressor attenuates the slope toward zero. OLS reads
    \nbElevenFaceMe{} against a true 2.
  \item[Selection on the outcome.] Conditioning on a collider of treatment and outcome bends the
    slope. OLS reads \nbElevenFaceSel{} against a true 1.
\end{description}
%
An instrument is the cure whenever a clean source of variation, one that moves the treatment but
nothing else, can be found or designed.

\input{build/floats/nb11_faces}
